patient.communicationStyle: Struggling to speak due to throat tightness. Voice is hoarse. Clearly frightened. Partner/friend may provide collateral information.

responses.opening: I ate shrimp about 20 minutes ago and I feel terrible — my lips swelled up, I'm covered in hives, and my throat feels like it's closing. I'm having a really hard time breathing.
responses.severity: Really bad. I feel like I can't breathe properly. My throat is tight. I'm dizzy.
responses.onset: About 20 minutes after I ate shrimp at a restaurant. The hives came first, then my lips started swelling, then my throat.
responses.character: My throat feels swollen — like it's squeezing shut. And I'm so itchy everywhere.
responses.worse: It's getting worse every minute. The throat tightness keeps getting worse.

chiefComplaint.onset: 20 minutes after shellfish ingestion, rapidly progressive
relieving: nothing attempted yet — came straight to ED

present.name: diffuse urticaria
present.patientSays: I've got welts all over my body — arms, chest, legs, everywhere.

present.name: angioedema (lips, tongue)
present.patientSays: My lips are huge. My tongue feels thick.

present.name: throat tightness/dysphagia
present.patientSays: My throat feels like it's closing. I can barely swallow.

present.name: stridor/hoarse voice
present.patientSays: Can you hear my voice? It sounds weird. It's getting harder to talk.

present.name: dyspnea/wheezing
present.patientSays: I can't take a full breath. Something's wheezing inside my chest.

present.name: lightheadedness
present.patientSays: I feel really dizzy, like I might pass out.

present.name: abdominal cramping/nausea
present.patientSays: My stomach is cramping too. I feel like I'm going to throw up.

present.name: generalized pruritus
present.patientSays: I'm itchy everywhere. It's driving me crazy but I'm more worried about breathing.

absent.name: prior anaphylaxis
absent.patientSays: I've never had anything like this before. I've eaten shrimp before with no problem... well, actually, last time I did get a little itchy but it went away.

absent.name: sting or injection
absent.patientSays: No, nobody stung me. It was the shrimp.

medications.name: Cetirizine
medications.patientSays: I take Zyrtec sometimes for allergies.

socialHistory.alcohol: Social — had a glass of wine with dinner tonight

present: atopic history (eczema, seasonal allergies, childhood asthma)
prior mild reaction to same trigger (progressive sensitization)

physicalExam.general: Young adult in severe distress, sitting upright, audible stridor, diffusely urticarial, lip/tongue angioedema visible
physicalExam.heent: Marked angioedema of lips, tongue, and uvula. Oropharynx edematous. Voice is hoarse. Audible inspiratory stridor.
physicalExam.pulmonary: Diffuse bilateral expiratory wheezes. Decreased air movement. Stridor on inspiration. Using accessory muscles.
physicalExam.abdominal: Diffuse mild tenderness, hyperactive bowel sounds
physicalExam.skin: Confluent raised urticarial wheals over trunk, extremities, and face. Erythematous. Warm. Angioedema of face/lips.
physicalExam.extremities: No edema, mild urticaria on dorsal hands

labs.note: Elevated — supports mast cell degranulation / anaphylaxis diagnosis
Mild eosinophilia consistent with atopic background
Mild hypoxemia, respiratory alkalosis from tachypnea

imaging.findings: Hyperinflation. No focal consolidation or pneumothorax.

onCorrectTreatment.vitalsTrend: BP improves to 110s/70s after epinephrine and IV fluids. HR decreases to 90s. SpO2 improves to 97% on 2L NC.
onCorrectTreatment.symptomsTrend: Urticaria fading within 30-60 minutes. Angioedema slowly resolving. Stridor resolved after first IM epi dose. Wheezing improved after nebulized albuterol.
onCorrectTreatment.narrative: IM epinephrine 0.3mg given immediately on arrival. IV access established, 1L NS bolus running. Diphenhydramine 50mg IV and famotidine 20mg IV administered. Nebulized albuterol. Methylprednisolone 125mg IV. Patient monitored for 4-6 hours for biphasic reaction. Discharged with EpiPen prescription and allergist referral.

onDelayedTreatment.vitalsTrend: BP drops to 70s/40s, HR 140s, SpO2 82%, stridor worsens
onDelayedTreatment.narrative: Airway edema progresses to near-complete obstruction. Difficult intubation due to edema. Requires surgical airway. Refractory hypotension requiring epinephrine drip and aggressive fluid resuscitation.

keyLearningPoints: Anaphylaxis diagnostic criteria: acute onset + 2+ organ systems involved (skin, respiratory, cardiovascular, GI)
EPINEPHRINE is the FIRST-LINE treatment — not antihistamines, not steroids. IM 0.3-0.5mg in anterolateral thigh
There is NO contraindication to epinephrine in anaphylaxis — the risk of NOT giving it always outweighs side effects
Biphasic reactions occur in ~5-20% — observe 4-6 hours minimum
Tryptase drawn at presentation (and at baseline later) helps confirm diagnosis retrospectively
EVERY patient with anaphylaxis goes home with an EpiPen prescription and allergist referral
mustNotMiss: Airway compromise — assess for stridor, voice changes, tongue/uvula swelling FIRST
Cardiovascular collapse — anaphylactic shock requires epinephrine + fluids + positioning
Biphasic reaction — can occur 1-72 hours later, typically 4-12 hours
commonPitfalls: Giving antihistamines INSTEAD of epinephrine — antihistamines treat symptoms, not the underlying mediator cascade
Giving epinephrine IV push instead of IM (risk of arrhythmia) — IV drip is reserved for refractory shock
Sub-therapeutic epinephrine dose — adults need 0.3-0.5mg IM, can repeat every 5-15 minutes
Discharging without EpiPen prescription or allergist referral
Not recognizing atypical presentations (GI-predominant anaphylaxis, or anaphylaxis without skin findings — occurs in ~10-20%)
Not observing for biphasic reaction — minimum 4-6 hour observation

expertDifferential.why: Known allergen exposure + rapid multisystem involvement (skin + respiratory + cardiovascular + GI) = meets diagnostic criteria
Skin involvement alone, but respiratory compromise and hypotension push to anaphylaxis
Wheezing present, but diffuse urticaria and angioedema indicate systemic allergic process
Hypotension and lightheadedness, but bradycardia expected (not tachycardia), and no rash/bronchospasm

teachingHooks.ifGivesAntihistamineFirst: What drug should come first in anaphylaxis? Epinephrine is ALWAYS first-line. Antihistamines help itching but do not reverse bronchospasm, angioedema, or hypotension.
teachingHooks.ifHesitatesOnEpinephrine: There is no contraindication to epinephrine in anaphylaxis. A brief tachycardia or tremor from epi is vastly preferable to airway loss or cardiovascular collapse.
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutPriorReactions: Key insight — the patient had a 'mild itch' with prior shellfish exposure. This is a sentinel event — progressive sensitization means each subsequent exposure can be worse.
teachingHooks.ifMissesGISymptoms: GI involvement (cramping, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) counts as a separate organ system for anaphylaxis criteria.
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutSteroidRole: Steroids may help prevent biphasic reactions but take 4-6 hours to work. They are ADJUNCTIVE, not primary treatment.
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutTryptase: Tryptase peaks 1-2 hours after onset. Draw at presentation and again at baseline (24+ hours later). Ratio >1.2x baseline + absolute >11.5 supports anaphylaxis.
